ocr.text: The analysis of maternal serum selenium levels provides critical insights into the nutritional status of expectant mothers and its
potential implications for both maternal and fetal health. In the presented data, the parameters of interest include the serum
selenium concentrations measured in nanograms per milliliter (ng/ml) across two distinct groups: patients and controls. The
mean serum selenium levels for the patient group ranged from 89.5 to 128 ng/ml, while the control group exhibited a slightly
higher range of 92 to 131 ng/ml. This observation raises important questions regarding the nutritional differences or health
conditions prevalent in each cohort. Furthermore, the P-value of 0.08 suggests a trend towards significance in the comparison of
selenium levels between the two groups; however, it falls short of conventional thresholds typically used to denote statistical
significance. These findings underscore the necessity for further investigation into the role of selenium in maternal health and
its potential influence on pregnancy outcomes. Understanding these dynamics could inform nutritional guidelines and
intervention strategies aimed at optimizing maternal and fetal well-being.
Parameters  Patients  Controls  P- value
Maternal serum selenium (ng/ml)  Range  89.5–128  92–131  0.08
Neonatal serum selenium (ng/ml)  Range  10–90  60.8–105.6  0.001b
S. selenium in mild HIE (ng/ml)  Range  61–90  60.8–90.2  0.05
S. selenium in moderate HIE (ng/ml)  Range  45–70  60.8–105.6  0.001b
S. selenium in severe HIE (ng/ml)  Range  10–49  60.8–105.6  0.001b
